sentences.text: Punish.
Yeah.
The punish colony.
Yes.
I was in this same compound, but we came there just to be isolated from the rest.
There were, as I told you, three blocks isolated in the main D camp, which was the Mannerlager, the men's camp, where most of the people and their jobs were going from this camp.
But in this D, or Mannerlager, where the head of the Mannerlager was a very bad criminal man, French-Danish.
And we knew him very well.
These three blocks where the Strafcommando, which was block number 13 and block number 11 and nine, which were the Sonderkommando-- they were closed.
And nobody could go in.
I mean, nobody would even like to go in because it meant to be put to punishment.
These men were like lepers.
Nobody could--
Well, they-- nobody should-- there shouldn't be any contact with them.
But we children came there because they wanted, somehow, we shouldn't mix with the other people for one reason or another.
We don't know till today what was their intention-- to keep us for the Red Cross, or to send us for exchange.
We don't know.
But anyhow--
That was the whole time, I mean, from July-- from the middle of July, when we came there, till the 18 of January '45 when we left Auschwitz.
From time to time there were selections.
But even the Sonderkommando who was living in these blocks and had to go daily to work in the crematorium-- later on, they had to live in the crematorium.
Mainly, that was the crematorium number one and number two.
To live there.
On the roof, there were some special railings for them built.
So they didn't-- well, they're very afraid.
They were busy and so on.
Yeah.
They-- we were witnessing the whole annihilation of the Hungarian Jews, I told you, from April, when we were still in the Familienlager.
And later on, we could see all the transports till the late autumn from Hungaria.
But me-- from time to time, came other transports from Paris, I remember-- I remember once came to our block, to the Strafcommando, straight from Champs-Elysees.
A young father and a young boy about 11 or 12 came to us.
And we didn't know why and so on.
Jews.
And we couldn't speak French.
But it was very strange.
From time to time, they came-- single people, for one reason or another.
Also, some other German, [NON-ENGLISH] they took from the gypsy camp.
But later on, block number nine and also 11-- when block 11 was a kind of little [INAUDIBLE] first aid, a laboratorium-- I mean, first aid when somebody broke or got something.
So they had their own doctor.
I remember he was from a Czech origin.
And even I, myself, when I got something on my knee, and they had they best medication, of course, which they took from the parcels from the people who had been gassed.
So they had the best things.
And they gave-- he gave me there, on this block number 11, the first aid.
But these two blocks were also emptied, selection and so on, all the rest of the people went to live in the crematorium.
And slowly, also the straf company was emptied.
In the end-- But I want to ask you about the straf.
Yeah.
And they came, all kinds of people.
Of course.
In our courtyard there was this famous--
In our courtyard.
It belonged to our block.
There was-- there were the gallows, portable gallows, and a kind of table for punishment.
And sometimes we witnessed when people, let's say, some people got the punishment for two or three months to be in the Strafcommando and 40 lashes and so on.
And this 40 lashes, they-- somebody had to count.
Sometimes the prisoner had to count it and the SS man or sometimes two SS men, but they were so tired from this job.
They were lashing them.
And sometimes we were allowed to see it.
Sometimes we had to be in the block, and we could look through the wood and see what's going on.
Well, I remember one prisoner tried to escape.
And he was caught.
And he was terribly tortured.
And I think I told you about it.
In the Strafcommando by SS.
And the huge stick, which we were carrying the barrels of soap, he broke on him.
And he was running.
And it was unbearable to look.
It was, even for us, who where hardened a bit-- terribly.
And I also remember once they caught three people.
One of them was a Russian by the name [NON-ENGLISH].
And he tried to commit suicide.
He cut his veins.
And that was, let's say, in the early morning.
And he had to be hanged in the afternoon when all the people came back from the work, which would be around 4 or 6 o'clock.
And this SS man said, this man had to live till the hanging.
And they brought the best equipment.
They sent a car and phone, I don't know what else, to bring a doctor and bandage--
--to keep him alive.
And I remember they schlepped him completely.
He was white and hardly could walk.
But they still hanged him.
And this hanging, all of us had to march-- it was before the kitchen.
There was a big-- in the beginning of the Mannerlager-- and near the kitchens, they put up some gallows, just the string.
And these three people were hanged.
And the orchestra had to play.
And that was his execution.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there were also Russian--
Yes, they came-- to us, well, again, unpredictable.
Let's say there was one SS man, Stefan Baretzki.
He was famous for his brutality.
And I saw many times how he finished some people-- some prisoners when he, for one reason or another, he didn't like them.
So he could beat them up that they couldn't get up anymore.
And most probably they were killed.
But on the other hand, these same SS men came to our block and even put up a ping pong table and played with us.

And the same Baretzki, once, in the evening, he just choose 10 people from the whole compound of the Mannerlager and said, come to the SS [NON-ENGLISH], which means to the gate, which is most dangerous.
We  knew what it meant, to be tortured, or killed, or something like that.
And he came out.
We were staying in a row, 10 different people.
I was one of them.
And he took a huge salami, cut it in 10 pieces, gave to each one, one piece, and said, go.
So--
Well, I remember more how afraid I was.
But the same Baretzki-- and also our block had [NON-ENGLISH] Bednarek-- much later, I think around '64, I witnessed--
In Frankfurt was the Auschwitz trial.
And I was there to witness.
And I--
--met again, Baretzki and Bednarek.
And I had to say what I know.
And by the way, I remember that all SS people denied that they have done this or that till the very end.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Of course I could recognize them.
And only Baretzki even repented.
He took-- he's the only one.
He was, at that time, a very young man.
He was very sorry.
And he said, yes, I have done it.
And he was sorry.
That's interesting.
From all other German--
At that time, he was a terrible criminal.
But I witnessed that even the worst SS men had, [INTERPOSING VOICES] here and there-- I mean, in the camp-- a kind of spark of humanity.
And maybe because we children were still looking a bit more--
--human than the other one, or maybe they-- we reminded them of somebody.
So they behaved differently.
Oh, yes.
We knew them.
And we talked with them.
And I even remember the songs or the music that [? to ?] play.
They were--
Usually Sundays, but always-- or nearly always when all the work was-- I mean, all the prisoners came back from work, or sometimes even when they went to work.
But Sundays, for the pleasure of the SS.
And I remember the song, [NON-ENGLISH] and other very kitschy Viennese and other German songs, plays.
And I remember later on, they were disbanded, and they had also to work in the-- or in a kind of Rollwagen [? commander. ?] But they were older.
I mean, we were children, and they were, let's say, in the late 20s, 30s.
Yeah, former musicians.
And now they had to do the same work like us.
I remember once, I met them on the way when we went to the crematorium for wood and so on.
And they were very badly treated.
I mean, they were really beaten up by the Germans and was no opportunity that-- if--
Yeah.
They-- maybe some of them survived.
I remember another woman who wasn't in this orchestra in Birkenau-- because I was in the Mannerlager.
But she had polio and was limping.
And she told me when she came to Auschwitz, Mengele saw her, that she was limping.
And I ask her what he said.
And she told him she had an operation.
And he was interested to know who was the doctor who performed the operation.
And he left her-- because of his so-called medical interest-- into the camp.
And her job was transcription of the notes for the orchestra in Auschwitz.
And she told me, when she came to Israel, she had here some relatives who came just before the--
--relatives-- before the war.
And when she came, her relatives said, oh, how strange that you survived.
Only whores could survive.
So that wasn't so pleasant.
Yeah.
Only somebody who sold himself.
Yes.
Well, now, the front is coming near.
And slowly the camp is--
--evacuating.
And we--
But not in the sense of really killing people.
No.
They're just taking them out on these marches.
Most of them were taken-- taken out, because I remember they [? weren't ?] gassing.
In the late autumn, it stopped.
In the very late autumn, there was not any more gassings.
They only burned in the crematorium some Musulmans they brought.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yeah, the reason was very simple.
We knew-- Musulman was an expression, a name in Auschwitz, it's nothing to do with Mohammed.
Somebody who lost the will to live and who is completely-- he doesn't care anymore what's happening around.
And of course, he loses, slowly, weight and doesn't care how he looks.
And in Auschwitz, it was most important to keep, somehow--
--clean, and because this was the very first one who were beaten up or sent to the crematorium, et cetera.
And these Musulmans, you could see, oh, he-- he's on the way to the crematorium-- or, as we called it in our children language, with one leg in the crematorium.
And we tried--
Not to look like it--
And not to be one.
We tried, thanks to the education we got from Theresienstadt and mainly from Fredy Hirsch.
He was very punctually on it that we should be always clean and correct.
Well, how-- what we have done all together in Auschwitz-- everyone tried to get out of heavy, physical work, which you couldn't endure.
And we were lucky that our work wasn't killing.
But even this work we had to do, everyone tried to work as less as possible.
And be aware.
Let's say we took breaks.
But in the minute we saw some SS, we said, achtzehn. 'Achtzehn' means 18, but it sounds like 'achtung,' beware.
So we suddenly shouted, achtzehn, which sounds like achtung.
And achtung means beware.
And then at once, of course, we pretended that we worked with all our might.
And it depended who was your kapo or who was supervising your work.
If he was good natured, so he, himself said, oh, pretend or show off that you work with the last-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that you do something.
You are working hard.
Because the danger was you are caught that you don't work.
But you have to be very clever to pretend.
And if you can, not to work at all.
And my philosophy--
Losing strength.
Yeah.
And I know, also, there was a time when we could choose to go on a transport, meaning that we have a choice-- as if a choice-- to go out of Auschwitz-- I mean, from the danger to be gassed.
But the danger-- our danger was that we will be-- we will be dying in the work--
--out work and hard work, or by-- be killed off by the SS men and so on.
I, because I was so weak and small, choose-- I said, as long as I am here, well, maybe I will end in the gas chambers, but this is only suffering for a comparatively short time.
But as long as I'm here, I know how to get enough food not to starve.
Whereupon to go out, I didn't know what will happen.
Most probably.
Well, what I was, at that time, is very curious.
It was just in puberty.
And I wanted to know what's going on.
And something was pushing me-- I have to remember.
And I was drawing, also, in the camp.
I had some papers from time to time with a pencil.
But of course, these drawings I had to hide or they were lost.
But I reconstructed them at once after the war-- at once when I was able to walk and to draw.
After I got my typhoid.
And I think it was in July '45, I made these drawings about the crematorium, et cetera, and the gas chambers.
Yes, I will show you afterwards.
Yeah.
Before the march, I would like to mention something.
Even the block 13 was liquidated.
Evacuated.
And we came to a different block, number 29, which was a children block, a Jugend block.
What happened-- there came so many transports from Hungaria, from Greece, some from Yugoslavia, a few partisans, and mainly from Slovakia.
They-- and the crematorium, at that time, was so busy with gassing all the Hungarian Jews that the overflow came to the camp, and mainly to our camp.
And here, suddenly, there was a new block of youngsters between, I would guess, 14 and 16, 17.
The kapo of this block, on block number 29, was Hans [?
Erlinger. ?] I even remember his numbers-- [NON-ENGLISH], 71 302.
He was a Jew?
No, he was a German or, I think, a political prisoner, but very kind and good to us children.
He was a sick man.
Later on, he accompanied us, even, to Mauthausen.
And he was extremely--
Hans Erlinger. ?] And he was very kind to all of the children.
And he took us with the Rollwagen.
And so and always somebody else had to accompany us.
Sorry?
Well, it depends from where they come.
Some-- some of the children came straight from Slovakia, so they were in better condition.
I remember some of these children once came to us, and I asked them, from where are you, and what happened to you?
And they said, well, we were just in the gas chambers.
We were naked.
And then came Schwarzhuber.
And the half of the children who were just entering the gas chambers-- said, out.
He doesn't know why, but he was-- this group of children were in the gas chambers.
The half went in.
And the half, Schwarzhuber in the last moment, took out.
By the way, there was always a competition, a kind, who is a better killer, Schwarzhuber or Mengele.
All of them made selection, Dr.
Thilo and Dr.
Klein.
But in our case, maybe I told you that we went twice through selection.
First, Schwarzhuber selected us.
We didn't-- and a few days later, Mengele was-- Schwarzhuber took his job.
So he made another selection of the same group.
But these Hungarian or Slovakian children, I don't know today, exactly--
They were sort of competing on making these selections.
It was--
Yes.
Well, depends, everyone had his-- yeah.
No, I wouldn't think so.
But Schwarzhuber was the head of--
--the whole Birkenau camp.
Yeah.
And Mengele was the head-- we called him, of course, the Death Angel.
And we saw him several times.
But he was the head of the medical and experiments, et cetera.
And these children also, later, got a similar job as ours.
But slowly, they were sent off--
To Germany.
We don't know where, but to different camps.
No, not to gas because later on, there was no more gassing.
Maybe some of them did or-- but I remember only this group of children who told me this story.
And later on, just before the 18 of January, we knew something would be happen because there was a great nervous-ity in the camp.
I even remember Christmas '44.
We had to take, with our Rollwagen, a Christmas tree to some of the SS compound, from one camp to another.
That was also one of our jobs.
But usually, the job was--
The Germans still had some fun, but they were--
--nervous.
We knew that something is happening.
We could-- in the last days, we could even hear the [? cannon, ?] the shelling from far away.
And our friend who had better position tried to provide us with better clothes, and this I told you, with shoes, et cetera.
When it happened--
Well, they were-- we saw around us.
One camp after the other was emptied.
We went still with the Rollwagen, I remember, to the abandoned [NON-ENGLISH], which was next to us.
And there was nobody there anymore.
And why do I remember it?
Because we went in the abandoned kitchen.
Now, only the Mannerlager were still few-- I mean, some maybe, a couple of thousand prisoners.
And we saw salt in the kitchen.
Salt.
And we took it in the camp, and we made business with--
Something very important.
No, not in such-- but suddenly, we saw a lot of salt.
And we brought it, and we sold it to other prisoners.
And for that we got, let's say, four cigarettes.
And four cigarettes was equivalent of a ration or a half of bread, or whatever we got.
I remember the cigarettes, by the way, a little before, were [?
Yosma ?] and Corfu because the Greek people who, at that time in summer '44, the Germans, I think, occupied Corfu or one of these islands, and these prisoners brought with them [?
Yosma ?] and Corfu.
I remember, even, a group of Greek Jews who came to our camp.
And it was difficult to speak with them because they spoke only Greek.
But we could somehow, in between us, understand each other.
By the way, once, to Theresienstadt, came a group of children from Bialystok--
--which were isolated.
And they had to live in Theresienstadt completely isolated.
And--
Why?
Because they came from Bialystok.
And people who had some contact with them told us they were terribly afraid to take a shower.
And they were starting to scream, 'Gas!
Gas!' And we only know that there were some volunteers who worked with them from Theresienstadt--
That had to be '43, autumn '43, before we came.
They knew, but therefore, they were isolated.
So that they wouldn't [? speak. ?]
But Fredy Hirsch somehow was caught with the children, and they said his punishment was that he had to go afterwards, in September, to Auschwitz.
But these children--
Yeah, yeah.
Illegal.
But there were some workers and doctors who volunteered to work with these children.
And later on, they were sent to the east.
And we know from the Sonderkommando that all these children and all the volunteers and doctors who--
I don't know.
Must be around 100, 200, to even more.
But this group--
Again, we don't know.
Most probably, they-- when they annihilated the ghetto, they had in mind to do something with the children, maybe for exchange.
And as I told you, the Red Cross, at that time, was interested in the ghetto, Theresienstadt.
And they were afraid the German Red Cross, they will need a cover, also for the people who went to the east.
And therefore, most probably, we were kept, the Czech family camp, and maybe also our group of children.
But that's unknown.
